---
title: "Chapter 5: Reproducing + auditing two published methods"
subtitle: "Cloned the official code for Lawley 2022 and DEEP-SEAM, ran end-to-end, found label leaks and evaluation-inflation issues in both"
sidebar: reproductions
---

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)

## Setup

Two canonical mineral-prospectivity-mapping publications, each
re-run from the authors' published code on the authors' published
data, then audited against the paper text. Both reproduce cleanly.
Both also report headline numbers that are partly
evaluation-inflated in ways the paper text doesn't acknowledge. The
audits document the gap concretely, with numbers per source of
optimism.

If you only have time for one chapter, this is probably it: I took
a published result seriously enough to reproduce it end-to-end, then
took the reproduction seriously enough to ask where the published
number came from.

![Where Lawley 2022's published continental Zn-Pb AUC of 0.983 comes from. Removing a label leak takes it to 0.959; switching from 1-D to 2-D spatial-block cross-validation takes it to 0.868; training on Australia + Canada and testing on the US (a cross-continent transfer) takes it to 0.709.](./data/derived/portfolio_charts/lawley_waterfall.png)

## Lawley et al. 2022: continental Zn-Pb prospectivity

**Paper.** Lawley et al. 2022 (*Ore Geology Reviews* 141, 104635)
trained a gradient-boosted-trees model to predict sediment-hosted
lead-zinc mineralization across the US, Canada, and Australia on a
5.16-million-hexagon Uber H3 data cube. Reported AUC: **0.983**.

**Reproduction.** I cloned SRI International's public
re-implementation
([DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines](https://github.com/DARPA-CRITICALMAAS/sri-ta3-baselines),
MIT license) and ran the `H3_MVT_GBM_preferred` configuration
end-to-end on a 32 GB laptop. Test AUC on the held-out fold:
**0.9965**. Reproduces the published number within 0.014.

**Audit.** Three sources of optimism, each measured:

| Source | AUC loss |
|---|---|
| Label leak: `Training_MVT_Occurrence` is in the feature set and arithmetically defines half the target | 2.4 pp |
| 1-D latitude-band CV doesn't isolate cells across longitude (2-D quantile-block CV vs 1-D) | 9.0 pp |
| Within-continent vs cross-continent transfer (US/Canada → Australia, the conservative direction) | ~16 pp |

After all three corrections the AUC range under proper validation
is **0.71 to 0.87**, not 0.983. The 0.87 number under 2-D spatial
blocking is real signal. The proximity-to-black-shale,
proximity-to-passive-margin, and sedimentary-host-rock dictionary
features encode the MVT mineral system. The headline overstates the
claim to generalization. The reverse cross-continent transfer
(train Australia, test US/Canada) collapses to **AUC 0.557**, barely
above random.

**Engineering side-finding.** Reproducing the SRI pipeline on a 32
GB laptop required performance work. The published 7.2 GB CSV
inflates to about 25 GB in pandas. The published `neighbor_deposits`
helper used a row-wise `df.apply` plus an O(N²) list-not-in-list
dedup that ran for 42+ minutes without completing on the US +
Canada subset. The published `convert_categorical` used
`df.replace(dict)` and took about 5 minutes per string column. I
applied three fixes (streaming CSV to parquet via pyarrow,
vectorized `neighbor_deposits` using `explode + isin + groupby`,
`pd.factorize` for categorical encoding) that brought end-to-end
runtime to **3 minutes**. The vectorized `neighbor_deposits` is a
lower-bound ≥130× speedup on the 3.6 M-row US + Canada subset, and
the CSV-to-parquet load went from hours of swap-bound thrashing to
5 seconds.

## DEEP-SEAM Luo 2026: Curnamona REE prospectivity

**Paper.** Luo et al. 2026 (*Geoscientific Model Development*
19:2593) introduced DEEP-SEAM, a semi-supervised
deep-anomaly-detection architecture (deviation network from Pang et
al. 2019, plus heavy upstream feature engineering) for mineral
prospectivity. Reported top-2% capture on the Curnamona REE dataset
(7 known deposits across 870 feature cells): **86%**, with the
deviation network beating Random Forest by a 2-3× factor.

**Reproduction.** I re-implemented the deviation network in PyTorch
from the paper alone (Pang 2019 plus Luo 2026), without consulting
the authors' code. Under leave-one-out training on the seven
positives, the re-implemented model places three of seven held-out
positives in the top 1% of cells and matches the published top-30%
number. Curnamona top-1% capture: 43%, vs Random Forest's 0%. The
qualitative finding reproduces.

**Audit.** I then cloned the official EarthByte release
([MPM_Curnamona_REE](https://github.com/EarthByte/MPM_Curnamona_REE),
MIT license, Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.17098677) and found three
issues:

| Issue | Implication |
|---|---|
| The 86% top-2% capture is measured under `sklearn.train_test_split(test_size=0.3, stratify=labels)`, i.e. **0-D random splitting with no spatial blocking at all** | The published number is in-sample-like recall, strictly more permissive than Lawley's 1-D scheme |
| The Gaussian reference distribution in the deviation loss is `K.variable(np.random.normal(...))`, **initialized once at module load and frozen** instead of sampled fresh per batch as Pang 2019 specifies | The implemented loss is a deterministic margin loss instead of the stochastic-reference loss the paper describes |
| `confidence_margin = 20` instead of Pang 2019's default of `5` | A hyperparameter the paper doesn't explicitly call out |

## Cross-cutting methodology checklist

Both audits surfaced findings via the same questions. Worth
applying to any future MPM reproduction:

1. **What spatial-block scheme does the published evaluation use?**
   0-D (random), 1-D (latitude band), 2-D (lat-lon block), region
   transfer, or LOO. Each level up tightens the leakage. Lawley
   uses 1-D; DEEP-SEAM uses 0-D.
2. **Are the input features computed in a way that can be evaluated
   separately from the target?** Lawley's `Training_MVT_Occurrence`
   arithmetically defines half the target.
3. **Is the loss function as implemented the same as the loss
   function as described?** DEEP-SEAM's frozen reference variable
   was the concrete instance.
4. **Are hyperparameters consistent between paper and code?**
   DEEP-SEAM's `confidence_margin = 20` against Pang's 5.
5. **What does `predict_proba` actually return?** Calibrated
   probability, normalized rank, raw score, sigmoid of normalized
   rank?

## What this implies for the field

Both papers report headline numbers that are reproducible but
partly inflated by evaluation choices the paper text doesn't fully
acknowledge. The published geological insight is intact in both
cases: the features encode real mineral-system signal, and the
architectures do real work. The overstatement is in the claim to
generalization. For an exploration team, the 0.71-0.87 range is the
number that matters because it tells you what to expect on cells
the model has not seen. The 0.983 is closer to in-sample recall.

The next chapter ([cross-region experiments](cross_region.qmd))
extends this finding by testing whether DEEP-SEAM's published
advantage transfers to other deposit types and other REE provinces.
It doesn't.

## Deeper notebooks

- [DEEP-SEAM official-code audit](./notebooks/prospectus/deep_seam_audit.html): comparison of EarthByte's TensorFlow implementation against the paper text plus my PyTorch port.

[← back to portfolio map](index.html)
